# Budgeting

<br>

A budget is a plan for the money you expect to receive. The goal isn't to restrict yourself -- it's to make spending intentional.

## What this chapter covers

  - Fixed vs. variable expenses
  
  - Zero-based vs. percentage budgets
  
  - Common budgeting tools and spreadsheets

## What is a budget?

## Savings Rate

Your savings rate matters more than your income or investment returns, especially early on. Someone earning $60,000 who saves 20% will often outpace someone earning $120,000 who saves 5%.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: 'Conscious Spending Plan'
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'look': 'handDrawn', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart LR
    Income(["Monthly Income"]) --> Taxes["Taxes"]
    Income --> FixedCosts["Fixed Costs<br/>50-60%"]
    Income --> Investments["Investments<br/>10%+"]
    Income --> SavingsGoals["Savings Goals<br/>5-10%"]
    Income --> GuiltFreeSpend["Guilt-Free Spending<br/>20-35%"]
    
    FixedCosts --> Rent("Rent/Mortgage")
    FixedCosts --> Utilities("Utilities & Insurance")
    FixedCosts --> Groceries("Groceries & Transport")
    FixedCosts --> MinDebt("Minimum Debt<br>Payments")
```

This is Ramit Sethi's **Conscious Spending Plan**—a flexible framework rather than a rigid budget.

## The Four Buckets

The table below displays the 'four buckets' or categories your income should be split into. 

| Bucket | Target % | Purpose |
|--------|----------|---------|
| **Fixed Costs** | 50–60% | Rent, utilities, groceries, transport, minimum debt payments |
| **Investments** | 10%+ | 401(k), Roth IRA, index funds (pay your future self first) |
| **Savings Goals** | 5–10% | Emergency fund, vacations, down payment, gifts |
| **Guilt-Free Spending** | 20–35% | Dining out, hobbies, clothes—enjoy life now |

## Living Within Your Means

> "*Spending money to show people how much money you have is the fastest way to have less money.*" — [Morgan Housel, *The Psychology of Money*](https://www.goodreads.com/quotes/10594676-spending-money-to-show-people-how-much-money-you-have)

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: 'Automate to keep it simple'
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'look': 'handDrawn', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart TD
    Start(["Take-Home Pay"]) --> PayYourselfFirst{"<strong>Automate First</strong>"}
    PayYourselfFirst --> InvestAuto["Auto-transfer<br>to Investments"]
    PayYourselfFirst --> SaveAuto["Auto-transfer<br>to Savings"]
    InvestAuto --> Remainder("What's Left =<br> Your Spending Limit")
    SaveAuto --> Remainder
    Remainder --> SpendFreely(["Spend Freely<br>Within This<br>Amount"])
```

**The trick:** Don't budget what's left after spending. Spend what's left after saving and investing. This single mental shift changes everything.

## A simple starting framework: 50 / 30 / 20

  - **50%** needs (housing, food, utilities, minimum debt payments)
  - **30%** wants (dining out, entertainment, travel)
  - **20%** savings & extra debt repayment

```{r}
#| label: budget-r
#| code-fold: true
income <- 5000
data.frame(
  Bucket = c("Needs (50%)", "Wants (30%)", "Save/Debt (20%)"),
  Amount = c(income * 0.50, income * 0.30, income * 0.20)
)
```

```{python}
#| label: budget-py
#| code-fold: true
income = 5000
{
    "Needs":    income * 0.50,
    "Wants":    income * 0.30,
    "Save/Debt": income * 0.20,
}
```
